questions.question: A radiography crew stops for lunch and parks where the truck is visible through the diner window. The loaded camera is locked but sitting loose in the open bed. Under 10 CFR 20.1801–20.1802, this arrangement is:
options: acceptable — the locked camera by itself satisfies the storage requirement
acceptable — keeping the truck in view through the window counts as constant surveillance
a violation — the source is neither under constant surveillance nor secured in storage, and the regulations recognize no third state
acceptable for stops shorter than one hour under the short-trip exception
questions.explanation: Part 20 allows exactly two states: material not in storage needs control and constant surveillance (20.1802) — eyes on and able to intervene — while material in storage must be secured against unauthorized removal (20.1801). A locked camera loose in an open bed is not secured (a Category 2 source would also need the two barriers and disabled vehicle of 37.53), and a glance through a diner window is not direct control. This lunch-stop condition is precisely how sources get stolen.

questions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.29, the physical inventory of licensed material must be performed:
options: quarterly, accounting for every sealed source and every depleted-uranium device on the license, including the location of each
monthly, but only for sources currently assigned to field crews
annually, at the same time as instrument calibrations
semiannually, covering sealed sources only — depleted-uranium shielding is exempt
questions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.29 requires a quarterly physical inventory that accounts for all sealed sources and all devices containing depleted uranium, including where each one is located. Together with the utilization log of 34.71 — which tracks each source's device, assigned radiographer, site of use, and dates out of and back into storage — it is how a licensee proves nothing has gone missing between jobs.

questions.question: A permanent vault holds three Ir-192 cameras loaded with 60 Ci, 30 Ci, and 25 Ci sources behind a single vault door. For 10 CFR Part 37 categorization, the licensee must treat this as:
options: three separate Category 2 quantities, each evaluated on its own
a single aggregated quantity of 115 Ci, because breaching one barrier would reach all three sources — collocated activities are summed against the Appendix A thresholds
only the largest source, 60 Ci, since a thief could carry away just one camera
exempt from Part 37, because aggregation applies only to material in transit
questions.explanation: Part 37 aggregates collocated sources: when breaching a single barrier would reach several sources, their activities are summed. Each camera here already exceeds the Ir-192 Category 2 threshold of 0.8 TBq (21.6 Ci), and the vault's security measures must address the 115 Ci total — stack enough Category 2 cameras behind one door and a vault can climb into Category 1 obligations.

questions.question: A vault's shielding was verified by survey when the company owned two cameras. The company grows and now stores six loaded cameras there. What does 10 CFR 20.1301 require?
options: Nothing further — vault shielding is approved once, at construction
Post the vault as a High Radiation Area to cover the increased inventory
Reclassify the adjacent office as a restricted area so the public limits no longer apply
Re-survey to confirm every adjacent unrestricted area still stays within 2 mrem in any one hour and 100 mrem in a year
questions.explanation: Vault shielding is sized by the limits next door: every adjacent unrestricted area — the office behind the wall, the sidewalk, the floor above — must stay within the 10 CFR 20.1301 public limits of 2 mrem in any one hour and 100 mrem in a year. Those limits are proven by survey, and a vault adequate for two cameras is not automatically adequate for six, so surveys are repeated whenever the stored inventory grows in number or activity.

questions.question: Which of these gamma sources could legally be offered for transport in a Type A package (UN3332) rather than a Type B?
options: An Ir-192 source decayed to 20 Ci — below the Ir-192 A1 value of 1 TBq (27 Ci)
A fresh 100 Ci Ir-192 working source
A 15 Ci Co-60 source — Co-60's A1 value is 0.4 TBq (10.8 Ci)
None — special form sealed sources must always travel in Type B packages
questions.explanation: A1 is the maximum special form activity a Type A package may carry: 1 TBq (27 Ci) for Ir-192 and 0.4 TBq (10.8 Ci) for Co-60. A 20 Ci Ir-192 source sits below its A1 and may move as UN3332 in a Spec 7A Type A package, while the 100 Ci Ir-192 and 15 Ci Co-60 sources both exceed their A1 values and require an accident-tested, NRC-certified Type B package (UN2916). Special form by itself does not force Type B — activity above A1 does.

questions.question: Your finished, locked package surveys at a maximum of 110 mrem/h on the surface and 3.42 mrem/h at one meter. How is it labeled?
options: Yellow-II with TI 3.4, since the surface reading is below 200 mrem/h
Yellow-III with TI 3.4 — the one-meter reading rounds to the nearest tenth
Two RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III labels on opposite sides with TI 3.5 — the one-meter reading rounds up to the next tenth, and both criteria demand Yellow-III
White-I — a properly shielded Type B package never requires a Yellow label
questions.explanation: The transport index is the maximum one-meter reading in mrem/h rounded UP to the next tenth, so 3.42 becomes 3.5 — never rounded down or to the nearest. A 110 mrem/h surface reading exceeds the Yellow-II surface ceiling of 50 mrem/h, and a TI above 1.0 exceeds the Yellow-II TI ceiling; either alone forces Yellow-III. Two completed labels — contents, activity in SI units, and TI — go on opposite sides, and the Yellow-III label puts RADIOACTIVE placards on all four sides of the vehicle.

questions.question: A newly certified Level II holds a current IRRSP card and is asked to prepare and sign shipping papers for source shipments. Under 49 CFR 172.704, the radiographer:
options: must first complete hazmat employee training — general awareness, function-specific, safety, and security awareness — with recurrent training at least every three years; the IRRSP card does not substitute
may sign immediately — radiation safety certification includes hazmat authority
needs hazmat training only for shipments by air or rail, not highway
may sign indefinitely under the RSO's verbal supervision without formal hazmat training
questions.explanation: Anyone who prepares, offers, or transports hazardous material is a hazmat employee under 49 CFR 172.704 and must complete general awareness, function-specific, safety, and security awareness training before performing those duties unsupervised, with recurrent training at least every three years. An IRRSP card is radiation-safety credentialing, not hazmat training — they are separate obligations, and signing shipping papers without DOT training puts the company in violation before the truck leaves the yard.

questions.question: A source changer arrives with papers reading TI 2.1, but your receipt survey measures 6 mrem/h at one meter — still below the transport-index ceiling of 10. The correct response is to:
options: accept the package — no absolute regulatory limit has been exceeded
stop and get the RSO — a reading far above the certified TI means something has shifted inside the package, even though no absolute limit is broken
apply a corrected label reading TI 6.0 and continue the receipt
have the carrier return the package to the shipper for investigation
questions.explanation: The one-meter reading cross-checks the label: a measured 2.1 against a labeled TI of 2.1 says the shipment arrived as certified, while 6 against 2.1 says the shielding or source position changed in transit. The 20.1906(d) notification triggers — the 71.47 limits of 200 mrem/h at the surface and TI over 10 — have not been reached, but a package that disagrees with its own certification stops where it is until the RSO directs the next step. Relabeling hides the evidence, and re-shipping a misbehaving package puts it back on the public highway.

questions.question: During receipt of a sealed-source package, the removable-contamination wipe is performed correctly by:
options: wiping the entire package surface and counting the smear immediately beside the crate to save time
wiping about 30 cm² and assuming the smear collected 100 percent of the surface activity
skipping the count whenever the package is labeled Yellow-III, since high external readings would mask the result

wiping about 300 cm² of the surface, counting the smear in a low-background area away from the package, and assuming 10 percent collection efficiency unless the program has determined the actual value
questions.explanation: The standard method wipes roughly 300 cm² with moderate pressure, then counts the smear with an appropriate instrument away from the package — a smear counted beside a Yellow-III crate measures the crate, not the smear. Unless the program has measured its actual collection efficiency, 49 CFR 173.443(a) has you assume the wipe picked up only 10 percent of what was on the surface. The result is compared against 4 Bq/cm² (about 220 dpm/cm²) for beta-gamma emitters, averaged over the area wiped.

questions.question: Your shop's replacement 100 Ci Ir-192 source was due Monday; by Wednesday the carrier's trace concludes the shipment is lost. Under 10 CFR 37.81, the shipping licensee must report the loss to the NRC Operations Center within:
options: 1 hour — the deadline for any lost licensed material
4 hours of determining that a Category 2 shipment is lost or missing — Category 1 shipments get only 1 hour
24 hours, by written report
30 days, in the annual material-status report
questions.explanation: 100 Ci of Ir-192 is far above the 0.8 TBq (21.6 Ci) Category 2 threshold, and 10 CFR 37.81 gives the shipping licensee 4 hours to report a lost or missing Category 2 shipment to the NRC Operations Center; Category 1 quantities allow only 1 hour. The receiving licensee's prompt call to the shipper when an expected source fails to arrive is what starts the trace — and that clock.
